chunk_summary: Facility-based therapeutic feeding for severe acute malnutrition (SAM) involves structured care in NRCs, using phased feeding protocols (F-75, F-100) to stabilize and rehabilitate children, with emphasis on monitoring and caregiver follow-up.
learning_objectives: Understand the phased feeding protocols (F-75 and F-100) used in facility-based management of SAM.
Recognize the clinical criteria for transitioning from stabilization to rehabilitation phases in SAM care.
Identify key monitoring parameters to prevent refeeding syndrome and ensure safe nutritional recovery.
Learn the importance of discharge planning and community-based follow-up to reduce relapse rates.
key_facts: Facility-based care for SAM is often delivered in Nutritional Rehabilitation Centers (NRCs) linked to pediatric wards.
The stabilization phase uses F-75 formula (~130 mL/kg/day) in small, frequent feeds to manage hypoglycemia, hypothermia, and infections.
Transition to F-100 or equivalent catch-up feeding occurs after clinical stabilization, appetite return, and absence of worsening edema.
Monitoring includes phosphate, potassium, magnesium, and provision of thiamine and multivitamins during feed advancement.
Structured protocols prevent harms like premature high-calorie feeding or prolonged underfeeding.
Discharge planning links caregivers to follow-up and community support to reduce relapse.
reference_quote: The stabilization phase prioritizes treatment of infection, hypoglycemia, hypothermia, and electrolyte imbalance while providing cautious feeding with a low-protein, low-sodium starter formula (F-75).
document_key_topics: Facility-based management of severe acute malnutrition
Monitoring and prevention of refeeding syndrome
